parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I used to just brush off this tight knot in my chest whenever things got quiet between us, but today it feels completely different. It sits right behind my ribs like a stubborn stone that refuses to budge no matter how many deep breaths I take. I keep telling myself it is just a passing mood, but the silence in the room feels heavier than usual. Every little shift in the atmosphere makes my shoulders tense up automatically. I just wish I could figure out why my body is reacting so strongly to something that never used to phase me at all.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like your body is trying to tell you something that your mind isn't quite ready to put into words yet. 

There is a specific kind of exhaustion that comes when a "passing mood" starts to feel like a permanent fixture. When that "knot" turns into a "stone," it usually means the situation has shifted from something you can tolerate to something that is costing you a lot of internal energy to ignore.

The fact that the silence feels heavier today suggests that the silence is no longer just an absence of noise—it’s becoming a presence of its own. When we are in a space where we don't feel fully settled or secure, our nervous system stays on high alert. That tension in your shoulders is your body’s "bracing" mechanism; it’s physically preparing for a conflict, a disappointment, or a change.

Sometimes our bodies react strongly to things that "never used to phase us" because we’ve simply run out of the capacity to carry them. You might have been able to brush it off before because you had more resilience or hope at the time, but now, the weight of that silence has accumulated.

If you feel comfortable sharing, has anything specific changed in the dynamic lately, or does this feel more like a slow build-up that finally reached a breaking point today?

turns.user_message: That heavy pressure in my chest has stopped being a rare visitor and now shows up almost every single evening. I catch myself holding my breath whenever the mood shifts, waiting for the other shoe to drop even when nothing actually happens. It is exhausting to constantly monitor the air around me just to keep the peace. I try to push through it by distracting myself, but the tension just builds up until I feel completely drained. I really hate how my own body keeps sounding the alarm over things I should probably just let slide.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining to live in a state of constant "high alert," especially when that pressure has moved from an occasional visitor to a daily presence. What you’re describing—the chest pressure, the breath-holding, and the hyper-vigilance—is a very real physiological response to chronic stress or emotional tension.

When you say you are "monitoring the air" to keep the peace, it suggests that you are carrying the heavy burden of managing not just your own emotions, but the emotional climate of your environment. That is a massive weight to bear. It makes sense that you feel exhausted; your nervous system is essentially running a marathon every single evening, scanning for threats and trying to prevent a "drop" that hasn't even happened yet.

Please try to be a little kinder to yourself regarding how your body is reacting. You mentioned hating that your body "sounds the alarm" over things you feel you should let slide, but your body isn't trying to annoy you—it’s trying to protect you. It has learned, for one reason or another, that these shifts in mood are "unsafe," and it is doing its best to prepare you for a hit. You can't "logic" your way out of a physical stress response once it’s already triggered.

Since this is happening every evening, have you noticed if there is a specific trigger or a certain time when the "monitoring" begins? Sometimes, just acknowledging to yourself, *"My body feels unsafe right now, even though I am physically okay,"* can slightly lower the volume of that alarm.

Are you in a position where you can step away for a few minutes when that pressure starts, or is the environment such that you feel you have to stay and manage it?

turns.user_message: The knot in my chest has grown so sharp that it actually makes it hard to sit still without pacing around the room. I feel completely trapped inside this cycle of anticipation, where every quiet moment turns into a heavy weight pressing down on my lungs. It is terrifying to realize that I am constantly bracing for impact even when everything seems perfectly calm. My whole system feels completely wired and raw, like I am running on empty while carrying something invisible that keeps getting heavier. I just want to finally exhale without feeling like the ground is about to shift beneath me again.
turns.model_response:
I can hear how exhausting and physically painful this is for you. That description of "bracing for impact" even in the quiet moments is a very visceral way to describe high-functioning anxiety or a nervous system that has been stuck in "survival mode" for too long.

When you are in that state, your body isn't just "worried"—it’s convinced there is a physical threat nearby. That’s why you feel the need to pace; your system is flooded with adrenaline and cortisol, and it’s trying to find a way to move that energy out of your limbs. It is incredibly draining to be "running on empty" while your engine is simultaneously redlining.

Since you mentioned it’s hard to sit still and you feel trapped, please know that it’s okay to let yourself move. Sometimes, trying to force yourself to sit still and "calm down" only makes the knot in your chest tighter because you’re fighting your body’s natural urge to discharge that energy.

If you feel up to it, I’m here to listen. Sometimes naming what you are "bracing" for—even if it’s just a vague sense of dread—can help peel back one layer of that weight. 

What does that "impact" feel like it might be? Is it a specific situation, or is it more of a general feeling that the "other shoe" is always about to drop?
